citation: Schubert, T., Friedmann, F., & Regenbrecht, H. (2001). The experience of presence: Factor analytic insights. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 10(3), 266-281.

SP.label: Spatial Presence
SP.description: Sense of being physically located inside the virtual environment.

items.text: I had a sense of acting in the virtual space.
I felt like I was actually there in the virtual environment.
I did not feel present in the virtual space.
I had a sense of being there.
I felt like the virtual environment surrounded me.

SP.scoring: Mean of items after reversing reverse-coded items. Range 0-6.

INV.label: Involvement
INV.description: Attention devoted to and involvement with the VE.

items.text: I was not aware of my real environment.
I still paid attention to the real environment.
I was completely captivated by the virtual world.
How aware were you of the real world surroundings while navigating in the virtual world?

INV.scoring: Mean of items after reversing reverse-coded items. Range 0-6.

REAL.label: Experienced Realism
REAL.description: Subjective experience of realism in the virtual environment.

items.text: The virtual world seemed real to me.
How real did the virtual world seem to you?
The virtual environment did not seem realistic.
How much were you able to examine objects closely?

REAL.scoring: Mean of items after reversing reverse-coded items. Range 0-6.

G.label: General Presence (single item)
G.description: Overall sense of being present - used as a global anchor.

items.text: How much did you feel like you were really there in the virtual environment?

G.scoring: Single item, range 0-6. Often used as validity check against subscale scores.

composite_score.method: Mean of SP, INV, and REAL subscale means (G excluded from composite).

reverse_coding_procedure: For reverse-coded items: reversed_score = max_scale - raw_score (i.e., 6 - raw)
notes: Validated across HMD, CAVE, and desktop VR systems.
SP subscale is the strongest predictor of behavioural presence measures.
G item correlates ~0.70 with SP; use for quick cross-check.
Normative data available at igroup.org for >20 VR systems.
Free to use for non-commercial research; cite Schubert et al. 2001.
